mechanism: Credit impulse leads equity cycle turns by 2-6 months through lending conditions affecting corporate investment and earnings. The credit impulse deceleration transmits through business investment, inventory, and consumption channels to corporate profits.
regime_dependence: Strongest when credit is the binding constraint. May be attenuated when fiscal deficits provide an alternative profit channel (Kalecki). Less informative during QE periods when credit supply is artificially unconstrained.

mechanism: Government deficits mechanically flow into corporate profits via the Kalecki-Levy identity. At 6-7% deficit/GDP, this channel sustains 7-15% of S&P 500 EPS, creating a fiscal floor under the cycle that delays or prevents typical credit-driven drawdowns.
regime_dependence: Proportional to deficit size. Currently at historically elevated deficit levels making the channel unusually strong. Would weaken significantly under fiscal consolidation (deficit from 6.5% to 4.0% GDP).

mechanism: Inflation regime determines whether stocks and bonds are positively or negatively correlated. Low stable inflation produces negative correlation (bonds hedge equities, lowering required ERP, supporting higher P/E). High or volatile inflation produces positive correlation, raising required ERP and compressing P/E by 3-5 turns.
regime_dependence: Regime transitions take 2-4 years. Currently in transition zone with inflation floor at 2.3-2.8% per iter_0023. If core inflation settles at 2.5-3.5%, correlation shifts positive and equilibrium P/E contracts to 15-20x.

mechanism: AI capex is deployed by the same concentrated cohort (Mag7/hyperscalers) that drives market concentration. Sustained capex maintains revenue growth and ROIC advantage, reinforcing concentration. Capex disappointment would trigger earnings disappointment in the concentrated cohort, unwinding concentration.
regime_dependence: Holds while hyperscalers maintain hedge-financed capex from FCF. Would break if AI revenue realization fails to reach 2-3x consensus TAM, converting the capex from value-creating to value-destroying.

mechanism: Passive flows are price-insensitive and mechanically allocate to the largest-cap names, reinforcing existing concentration. The inelastic markets hypothesis ($1 flow = $3-5 market cap) amplifies this, creating a Soros-ian reflexivity loop where concentration begets inflows begets more concentration.
regime_dependence: Strengthens as passive share increases (currently ~55%). Theoretical framework is sound but empirically untested — no passive-driven crash has occurred. Active managers may still set marginal prices.

mechanism: Small-caps are disproportionately sensitive to credit conditions due to higher leverage (3.0-3.5x vs 1.0-1.5x), higher floating-rate exposure (35-40% vs 15-20%), and lower interest coverage (~2.0x). The financial accelerator (Bernanke-Gertler-Gilchrist) transmits credit tightening more severely to smaller, more leveraged firms.
regime_dependence: Correlation with real fed funds rate of -0.65 to -0.75. Strongest during tightening cycles. The maturity wall concentration in 2025-2027 amplifies current sensitivity.

mechanism: The Kalecki channel creates fiscal dependency: current earnings embed $20-40/share from deficits. Fiscal consolidation mechanically removes this support, reducing EPS by $10-20/share (4-8%) under moderate scenarios or 15-20% under aggressive consolidation (deficit from 6.5% to 3-4% GDP).
regime_dependence: Risk is proportional to current deficit level and speed of consolidation. Currently at peak exposure with deficit at ~6-7% GDP. Godley sectoral balance framework projects private sector forced from surplus to deficit under consolidation.

mechanism: AI capex creates specific, quantifiable commodity demand shocks: 1.5-2.0 Mt copper, 150-200 TWh electricity, 3-5 Bcf/d natural gas. These physical constraints could become binding bottlenecks that slow capex execution or drive commodity inflation that compresses margins.
regime_dependence: Binds when physical supply cannot scale with demand. Copper supply constraints are tightest (mine development cycle 5-10 years). Power constraints depend on permitting and gas supply. Currently mixed commodity environment.

mechanism: Extreme concentration (30-38% top 10) creates mechanical divergence between cap-weighted and equal-weighted metrics. Cap-weighted P/E, ERP, stock-bond correlation, and earnings revision breadth all differ materially from equal-weighted equivalents, masking the cycle state of the median company.
regime_dependence: Divergence increases with concentration level. Currently at historically extreme divergence. Converges during concentration unwinds (historically via large-cap decline, not small-cap catch-up).

mechanism: ERP compression supports higher P/E multiples. At current levels (1.0-3.0% depending on methodology), ERP is below post-1990 median, placing forward 5-10 year returns in historically poor territory. The inflation/correlation regime determines whether ERP compression is rational (negative correlation environment) or mispriced.
regime_dependence: ERP compression is justified under negative stock-bond correlation (bonds hedge equities). If correlation regime shifts positive, ERP should expand, compressing P/E to ~18-19x.

mechanism: Yield curve inversion predicts recessions (7/7 record) which drive equity drawdowns. However, the signal requires decomposition: inversion from expected-rate compression signals recession, while inversion from term premium compression may not. The steepening trap means recession arrives during steepening, not inversion.
regime_dependence: Decomposition requirement is heightened in QT era where term premium behavior differs from pre-2008. ACM and Kim-Wright models diverge by 30-50bp, introducing model dependence.

mechanism: Earnings quality deterioration means non-GAAP earnings overstate true earnings power by 10-20%, making ERP calculations based on non-GAAP forward earnings artificially compressed. GAAP P/E of ~25-27x vs non-GAAP ~21-22x implies true ERP is lower than headline estimates.
regime_dependence: Worsens in late-cycle as management incentives to manage earnings increase. Accruals/total assets at ~4-5% vs ~2% early-cycle. SBC and non-GAAP adjustments are persistent features of current market structure.

mechanism: ROIC-WACC spread compression to 3.5-5.0pp creates nonlinear fragility: 100bp WACC increase destroys 25% of EVA at 4pp spread vs 12.5% at 8pp spread. This means rate shock pathway (pathway a) would have 2.5x larger impact than in 2017-19 conditions.
regime_dependence: Fragility increases as spreads compress. Current 3.5-5.0pp spread (with ±50bp uncertainty) is the tightest since pre-GFC. Would widen if AI capex delivers sufficient ROIC improvement.

mechanism: Sanctions and export controls create a structural wedge between geopolitically aligned and non-aligned EM markets. Aligned EMs benefit from supply chain diversification flows while non-aligned EMs face index deletion risk, compliance costs, and structural P/E discounts.
regime_dependence: Strengthens with geopolitical tension escalation. Weakens with de-escalation. Current alignment classification may shift over time.

mechanism: Late-expansion positioning means the economy is vulnerable to any of three cycle-ending pathways. Fiscal floor (condition 1 intact) delays but does not prevent eventual cycle turn. Minsky fragility (condition 3 met) and credit impulse approaching threshold (condition 2) indicate increasing vulnerability.
regime_dependence: Fiscal floor is the critical moderating variable. As long as deficits remain at 6-7% GDP, the fiscal Kalecki channel provides a floor. Pathway activation depends on which condition breaks first.
